nativeRange.habitat: Rocky shoreline of northern Lake Tanganyika; hugs cave walls and crevices in the littoral zone

decorPreferences: Extensive rock piles with caves, overhangs, and crevices for spawning and territory
PVC pipes or clay pots as additional spawning sites
Broken lines-of-sight between rock structures to reduce inter-family aggression
Open midwater swimming space between rock zones

diet.notes: Generally accepts high-quality cichlid pellets and frozen foods such as brine shrimp, mysis, Daphnia, and cyclops. Live foods are readily taken. A vegetable component (spirulina flakes, blanched spinach) is commonly recommended. Sinking or mid-water presentation preferred over surface foods.

goodWith: Conspecific family colony (offspring from the established pair)
Shell-dwelling Tanganyikans (Lamprologus ocellatus, Neolamprologus multifasciatus) in adequately sized tanks
Julidochromis or Altolamprologus in large tanks with sufficient territory breaks
avoidWith: Non-family conspecifics (highly aggressive toward unrelated N. brichardi)
Other Neolamprologus brichardi complex species (hybridization risk)
Malawi or Victorian cichlids (incompatible chemistry and temperament)
Small, timid fish that cannot escape territorial aggression
Fast, boisterous tankmates that stress the colony

summary: An elegant Lake Tanganyika cichlid famous for its distinctive lyretail fin extensions and remarkable colonial breeding system, where offspring remain with the family and help raise successive broods.
careNotes:
Neolamprologus brichardi is one of the most distinctive freshwater cichlids available to hobbyists, native to the rocky northern shores of Lake Tanganyika. Hard, alkaline rift-lake chemistry is essential — soft or acidic tap water needs buffering to replicate Tanganyikan conditions (pH 8.0–9.0, gH 9–25, kH 8–20).

This species is famous for its cooperative colonial breeding system: offspring from earlier spawns remain with the family group and actively help guard eggs and fry from subsequent broods. Over time, a single pair can develop into a multi-generational colony with clearly delineated family territories. Within the family unit, aggression tends to be low; however, unrelated conspecifics introduced into an established colony will be attacked aggressively. This population-explosion tendency is important to plan for — a successful colony will fill available space rapidly; surplus offspring will need periodic removal or rehoming.

A 150 L tank with a minimum 120 cm footprint is generally suggested for a pair; a colony setup benefits from 200 L or more to provide sufficient territory. Aquarists note that in inadequate space, overcrowding can cause the dominant pair to begin cannibalizing younger generations.

The species has also been treated taxonomically as Neolamprologus pulcher (the currently accepted scientific name in some authorities); the two names are used interchangeably in the hobby. Do not mix with other members of the brichardi complex (N. splendens, N. helianthus, N. marunguensis, etc.) as they hybridize freely.

Note on care level: sources describe this species as newcomer-friendly for Rift Lake cichlids, but the aggressive colony dynamics and need for regular population management make intermediate overall.
breedingNotes:
Breeding is generally considered easy once a bonded pair is established. Pairs spawn on the underside of cave roofs or crevice walls, with clutch sizes typically ranging from 50–120 eggs depending on the pair's maturity. Eggs hatch in approximately 2–3 days; fry are free-swimming by day 7 and will accept brine shrimp nauplii immediately. Sexual maturity tends to occur around 7 months of age at approximately 5 cm.

The standout feature is cooperative brood care: earlier-generation offspring remain in the territory and assist with guarding younger siblings. Successive spawns can occur every 2–6 weeks, leading to rapid colony growth. The main management challenge is population control — the colony can outgrow a tank quickly; a plan for rehoming surplus fish is advisable before starting a colony.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary source used in place of Aquarium Co-Op (no dedicated N. brichardi page found). SeriouslyFish provides comprehensive species-specific husbandry for this taxon.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (max 9 cm TL), temperature (22–25°C vs SeriouslyFish 23–27°C — used 23–27°C as aquarium-practice consensus), pH 8.0–9.0 (agreed), hardness 9–19 dH (vs SeriouslyFish 9–25 — used 9–25 to reflect hobby range). Order confirmed as Cichliformes.

additional.name: TFH Magazine
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (up to 10 cm cited, males typically 7–8 cm), temperature 25–26°C, tank size 151 L species-only minimum (confirmed 150 L rounded value). Breeding cycle 14–40 days between spawns; sexual maturity ~7 months at ~5 cm. Confirmed cooperative brood-care behavior.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave-spawning substrate brooder; lays 50–120 eggs on cave ceilings or rock crevices; eggs hatch in ~2–3 days, fry free-swimming by day 7. Cooperative colony care is the defining trait. Spawns every 2–6 weeks with established pairs. Population management is the primary keeper challenge.
